summary: Gruber examines the widespread problem of poorly designed HTML page titles across major websites. He argues that most big-name sites stuff their titles with SEO keywords, redundant category labels, and ugly punctuation that serves neither humans nor search engines well. He contrasts these cluttered titles with clean examples like Google and Apple, whose home pages simply use the company name. His core recommendation is that page titles should be written as good bookmark names — clean, scannable, and human-readable — using either 'Source: Headline' or 'Headline — Source' as the only sensible patterns.
key_points: HTML page titles serve multiple user-facing purposes: browser tabs, window titles, bookmarks, and tweet descriptions, making their design important
Most major news sites stuff titles with SEO keywords, redundant categories, and ugly punctuation separators like double colons
The only two sensible title patterns are 'Source: Headline' or 'Headline — Source' — everything else is junk
SEO-motivated title stuffing doesn't actually improve search rankings and actively hurts usability by making titles unreadable in tabs and bookmarks
The New Yorker's sloppy title punctuation is especially ironic given its reputation as the most precisely copy-edited publication in English
money_quotes: An awful lot of websites use patterns for page titles that are ugly, hard-to-scan, and/or just plain stupid.
The New Yorker — arguably the most precisely punctuated and copy-edited publication in the English-speaking world — would never use a colon like that in print.
It's like they're taking advice out of an SEO book from 1995.
That's a good rule of thumb for designing and writing page titles: pick a name that makes sense as the name of a bookmark for that page.
Write them for humans, not search engine spiders.

Putting SEO keywords in the page title doesn't actually help your page's rank in search engine indexes, and makes things harder for people trying to tweet a link, bookmark your page, or scan it from a list of currently open windows and tabs in their browser.
Trust the Googlebot to figure it out.
key_insight: Web page titles should be designed as clean, human-readable bookmark names — not as keyword-stuffed SEO bait that serves neither users nor search engines.
